section.title: The LORD Appears to Solomon
section.sectionRationale: After the temple's completion and dedication in chapters 6 to 8, the LORD appears to Solomon a second time, as He had at Gibeon, to answer the great dedicatory prayer directly. He confirms His Name upon the temple forever, but attaches a solemn condition: covenant faithfulness will establish Solomon's throne, while turning to other gods will bring exile and the temple's ruin. The chapter then surveys Solomon's other achievements at the end of the twenty year building project, his dealings with Hiram over the towns of Cabul, his use of forced labor and building projects across the land, and his fleet of ships trading gold from Ophir, closing the great central section on Solomon's reign before its unraveling begins in chapter 11.
section.thesis: The LORD graciously confirms His presence in the temple forever, yet ties Solomon's throne and Israel's future in the land to covenant faithfulness rather than to the temple's mere existence, so that the same chapter which crowns Solomon's achievements already sounds the warning that will explain Israel's eventual exile.
section.frameNote: Four units: the LORD's second appearance and the conditional promise, verses 1 to 9; Solomon's dealings with Hiram and the towns of Cabul, verses 10 to 14; Solomon's building projects and forced labor, verses 15 to 25; and Solomon's fleet and trade with Ophir, verses 26 to 28.

units.label: The LORD's second appearance
units.note: the conditional promise and the warning

verses.text: Now when Solomon had finished building the {{bayit|house}} of the LORD and the royal palace, and had achieved all that he had desired to do,
the LORD appeared to him a second time, as He had appeared to him at Gibeon.

And the LORD said to him: "I have heard your prayer and petition before Me. I have consecrated this temple, which you have built, by putting My {{shem|Name}} there forever; My eyes and My heart will be there for all time.
And as for you, if you walk before Me as your father David walked, with a heart of {{tamim|integrity}} and uprightness, doing all I have commanded you, and if you keep My statutes and ordinances,
then I will establish your royal throne over Israel forever, as I promised your father David when I said, 'You will never fail to have a man on the throne of Israel.'
But if indeed you or your sons turn away from following Me and do not keep the commandments and statutes I have set before you, and if you go off to serve and worship other gods,

then I will cut off Israel from the land that I have given them, and I will banish from My presence this temple I have sanctified for My Name. Then Israel will become an object of scorn and ridicule among all peoples.
And when this temple has become a heap of rubble, all who pass by it will be appalled and will hiss and say, 'Why has the LORD done such a thing to this land and to this temple?'

And others will answer, 'Because they have forsaken the LORD their God who brought their fathers out of the land of Egypt, and have embraced other gods, worshiping and serving them, because of this, the LORD has brought all this disaster upon them.'"

units.label: Solomon and Hiram, the towns of Cabul

verses.text: Now at the end of the twenty years during which Solomon built these two houses, the house of the LORD and the royal palace,
King Solomon gave twenty towns in the land of Galilee to Hiram king of Tyre, who had supplied him with cedar and cypress logs and gold for his every desire.
So Hiram went out from Tyre to inspect the towns that Solomon had given him, but he was not pleased with them.
"What are these towns you have given me, my brother?" asked Hiram, and he called them the Land of Cabul, as they are called to this day.
And Hiram had sent the king 120 talents of gold.

units.label: Solomon's building projects and forced labor

verses.text: This is the account of the forced labor that King Solomon imposed to build the house of the LORD, his own palace, the supporting terraces, and the wall of Jerusalem, as well as Hazor, Megiddo, and Gezer.
Pharaoh king of Egypt had attacked and captured Gezer. He had set it on fire, killed the Canaanites who lived in the city, and given it as a dowry to his daughter, Solomon's wife.
So Solomon rebuilt Gezer, Lower Beth-horon,
Baalath, and Tamar in the Wilderness of Judah,
as well as all the store cities that Solomon had for his chariots and horses, whatever he desired to build in Jerusalem, Lebanon, and throughout the land of his dominion.
As for all the people who remained of the Amorites, Hittites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites (the people who were not Israelites),
their descendants who remained in the land, those whom the Israelites were unable to devote to destruction, Solomon conscripted these people to be forced laborers, as they are to this day.
But Solomon did not consign any of the Israelites to slavery, because they were his men of war, his servants, his officers, his captains, and the commanders of his chariots and cavalry.
They were also the chief officers over Solomon's projects: 550 supervisors over the people who did the work.
As soon as Pharaoh's daughter had come up from the City of David to the palace that Solomon had built for her, he built the supporting terraces.
Three times a year Solomon offered burnt offerings and peace offerings on the altar he had built for the LORD, burning incense with them before the LORD. So he completed the temple.

units.label: Solomon's fleet and the gold of Ophir

verses.text: King Solomon also assembled a fleet of ships at Ezion-geber, which is near Eloth in Edom, on the shore of the Red Sea.
And Hiram sent his servants, sailors who knew the sea, to serve in the fleet with Solomon's servants.
They sailed to Ophir and imported gold from there, 420 talents, and delivered it to Solomon.

bayit.gloss: House. Verse 1 pairs the bayit of the LORD with Solomon's royal bayit as the two great projects now finished; the same word that named the temple throughout chapters 6 to 8 here frames the LORD's appearance as a direct response to the completion of both houses.

shem.gloss: Name. The LORD confirms He has consecrated the temple by putting His shem there forever, v3, the direct answer to Solomon's repeated prayer that God's Name would dwell in the house, 8:16-20, 29, 43-44, though the same chapter warns that persistent unfaithfulness will cause God to banish from His presence this temple sanctified for His shem, v7.

tamim.gloss: Integrity, blamelessness, wholeness. The LORD calls Solomon to walk before Him with a heart of tamim, as David his father walked, v4, the same standard of wholehearted covenant faithfulness David is repeatedly praised for and Solomon will later fail to keep, 11:4-6.

basic.context: After the temple is built and dedicated, the LORD appears to Solomon a second time, just as He had years earlier at Gibeon, chapter 3, to answer the prayer Solomon prayed at the dedication. God confirms that He has consecrated the temple and placed His Name there forever, but He immediately attaches a condition, Solomon's throne will be established only if he and his descendants walk faithfully before God; unfaithfulness will bring exile and the temple's destruction. The chapter then turns to a mixed report of Solomon's other achievements: an uneven land exchange with Hiram that leaves Hiram unimpressed, extensive building projects using forced labor from the remaining Canaanite peoples, and a profitable fleet of ships bringing gold from Ophir.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording a specific divine appearance and a summary of Solomon's administrative accomplishments; it is not a promise that every believer's obedience will be rewarded with earthly kingship or that disobedience always brings visible national disaster in the same form. What is directly instructive and enduring is the conditional principle itself, God's blessing on His people's institutions, then and now, is never detached from ongoing covenant faithfulness, and no religious structure, however magnificent, guarantees God's presence apart from obedient hearts.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD confirms He has placed His shem, Name, in the bayit, house, forever, directly answering Solomon's dedication prayer, yet He calls Solomon to walk with a heart of tamim, integrity or wholeness, the same standard David kept and Solomon will not always keep. The chapter's warning that God will banish the temple where He placed His shem shows that the Name's presence was always conditioned on faithfulness, not a permanent, unconditional guarantee bound to the building itself.
basic.typology: The LORD's warning that unfaithfulness would make the temple a heap of rubble anticipates Jesus' own warning that the temple of His day would be left desolate, Matthew 23:38, and finds ultimate contrast in Christ, the true and greater temple who cannot be destroyed by human unfaithfulness, John 2:19-21. Solomon's call to walk in tamim as David did points forward to the one greater than Solomon whose obedience was perfect and unbroken, fulfilling the covenant condition Israel's kings could not keep, Hebrews 4:15.
basic.christ: Where Solomon and his descendants were called to walk before God with integrity yet ultimately failed, bringing the very exile this chapter warns of, Jesus Christ walked before the Father in perfect, unbroken faithfulness, fulfilling the condition Israel's kings could not keep, Hebrews 4:15. He is the greater Son of David whose throne is established forever not conditionally but by His own perfect righteousness, Luke 1:32-33, and He is Himself the true temple, greater than Solomon's, which no unfaithfulness can cause to be destroyed or banished from God's presence, Matthew 12:6, John 2:19-21.
basic.god: God graciously answers prayer, confirming His Name and presence in the temple exactly as Solomon asked, yet He is not manipulated or contained by religious buildings; His covenant blessing always remains conditioned on wholehearted faithfulness. He is patient toward Solomon even while foreshadowing the discipline that unfaithfulness will eventually bring, showing that His warnings, like His promises, flow from covenant love rather than mere threat.
basic.crossReferences: First Kings 3 records the LORD's first appearance to Solomon at Gibeon, granting him wisdom, the appearance this chapter calls a second time. Deuteronomy 28 lays out the same blessings and curses tied to covenant faithfulness that frame the LORD's words here. Matthew 23:38 records Jesus warning that the temple of His own day would be left desolate, echoing this chapter's warning. And Hebrews 4:14 to 16 presents Christ as the one who kept perfect faithfulness where Israel's kings could not.
basic.oneStory: At the height of Solomon's achievement, God graciously confirms His presence in the temple, yet the same moment carries a solemn warning that will explain Israel's eventual exile centuries later, when the temple genuinely does become a heap of rubble. Solomon's own record of towns traded away and forced labor already hints that his heart is not entirely tamim, setting up the full unraveling in chapter 11 and pointing the whole story forward to the greater Son of David whose faithfulness never fails and whose kingdom cannot be shaken.

context.literary: The chapter divides cleanly between direct divine speech, verses 1 to 9, and a summary administrative report, verses 10 to 28, a pattern that lets God's covenant word frame and evaluate everything that follows; read this way, the account of towns traded away in an uneven exchange and the heavy use of forced labor reads less as neutral achievement and more as an early, subtle indicator of the compromises Solomon's later chapters will expose in full. The chapter closes the long central unit on the temple and palace, chapters 6 to 9, before chapter 10 turns to Solomon's fame and chapter 11 to his decline.
context.historical: A second divine appearance mirrors the first at Gibeon, 3:4-15, both bracketing Solomon's major building achievements with direct words from God. The Land of Cabul incident reflects real diplomatic relations between Israel and Tyre, a maritime power dependent on Israelite agricultural land, and Hiram's dissatisfaction, plus the wordplay on Cabul, probably meaning good for nothing, preserves an authentic, slightly embarrassing memory rather than idealized propaganda. The fleet at Ezion-geber and trade with Ophir, likely located in Arabia or East Africa, reflects the Red Sea trade routes known from other ancient Near Eastern sources, and the note about conscripted Canaanite remnant peoples matches the ongoing incomplete conquest described in Joshua and Judges.
context.canonical: This chapter's conditional promise directly answers Solomon's dedication prayer, chapter 8, particularly his own request that the LORD would establish David's line if his descendants walked faithfully, 8:25, making chapter 9 a divine ratification with a warning attached. The warning of exile and the temple's ruin anticipates the final chapters of 2 Kings, when Jerusalem falls and the temple is indeed destroyed, showing this chapter functions as a hinge between Solomon's glory and Israel's eventual judgment.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record the LORD's gracious confirmation of His presence in the newly built temple while establishing, in the clearest terms yet given to Solomon, that the throne's permanence and the temple's continuance depend on covenant faithfulness, not architecture, and to close the account of Solomon's building achievements with a summary that, read carefully, already shows small fractures beneath the surface splendor.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes specific historical events, a divine appearance, a land transaction, building projects, and a trading fleet; it does not prescribe forced labor, land exchanges, or naval commerce as models for believers or nations today. What is prescriptive and enduring is the theological principle voiced directly by God: blessing on His people's institutions remains conditioned on wholehearted covenant faithfulness, never on the mere existence of religious structures or achievements.

debates.question: Is the account of the towns of Cabul and Solomon's use of forced labor meant to cast an early shadow over his reign?

views.label: An early, understated warning sign
views.case: Placed immediately after God's conditional warning in verses 6 to 9, the report of an uneven exchange that leaves Hiram unimpressed and the conscription of remaining Canaanite peoples into forced labor reads as the narrator's characteristic technique of letting facts imply judgment; taken together with the extensive building projects and the fleet's gold, these details anticipate the excess and compromise fully exposed in chapter 11.

views.label: A straightforward, largely positive administrative record
views.case: Ancient Near Eastern kings regularly exchanged territory for services rendered, and using conquered or subjugated peoples for labor while protecting one's own citizens from servitude, verse 22, was considered prudent statecraft rather than a moral failing; the chapter may simply be documenting Solomon's substantial, largely successful administrative reach at this stage of his reign, before his later, more explicit failures.

debates.bearing: Either reading agrees that the chapter's placement, sandwiching this administrative report between God's explicit conditional warning and the following chapters' account of Solomon's wealth, wisdom, and eventual downfall, invites the reader to weigh every one of Solomon's achievements against the single question the LORD Himself raises, whether his heart remains wholly devoted.

study.originalLanguages: The LORD confirms that He has placed His shem, Name, in the bayit He has been given, the temple, forever, yet ties that permanence to Solomon walking with a heart of tamim, wholehearted integrity, exactly as David his father did. The same chapter's warning, that persistent unfaithfulness will cause God to banish from His presence the very temple sanctified for His shem, shows that the Name's dwelling was never an unconditional fixture bound to the building but a covenant relationship that could, tragically, be forfeited, precisely what later history in 2 Kings will record.

typology.type: The temple threatened with becoming a heap of rubble through unfaithfulness
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' own warning that the temple of His generation would be left desolate, Matthew 23:37-38, fulfilled in the destruction of AD 70.

typology.type: The call to walk before God with a heart of tamim, as David did
typology.fulfillment: Christ's perfect, unbroken obedience and integrity before the Father, fulfilling the covenant standard Israel's kings could not keep, Hebrews 4:15.

typology.type: The conditional establishment of Solomon's throne
typology.fulfillment: The unconditional, eternal throne secured for the greater Son of David through His own righteousness, Luke 1:32-33, Hebrews 1:8.

study.christ: The LORD's warning that unfaithfulness would leave the temple a heap of rubble finds its historical fulfillment in the destruction of Jerusalem, but its deeper answer in Christ, who both warned that the temple of His day would be left desolate, Matthew 23:38, and identified His own body as a greater temple that death itself could not finally destroy, John 2:19-21. Solomon is called to walk in tamim as his father David did, a standard he and his descendants would not sustain; Jesus alone kept it perfectly, Hebrews 4:15, so that the throne established conditionally for Solomon's line is at last secured unconditionally and forever for great David's greater Son, Luke 1:32-33.
study.god: God graciously confirms His presence and answers prayer, consecrating the temple by placing His Name there, yet He is never bound or contained by any structure; His covenant blessings remain conditioned on the wholehearted obedience of His people's hearts, not on buildings, wealth, or achievement. He disciplines in love, warning clearly before judgment ever falls, and He remains faithful to His larger purposes even when His people, including Solomon, ultimately fail to meet the standard He sets.

crossReferences.note: The LORD's first appearance to Solomon at Gibeon, granting him wisdom, which this chapter's second appearance directly recalls.
The blessings for obedience and curses for disobedience that frame the LORD's conditional words to Solomon in this chapter.
The eventual, literal fulfillment of the warning in verses 7-9, when the temple is indeed burned and Jerusalem falls.
Jesus warns that the temple of His generation would be left desolate, echoing this chapter's warning about unfaithfulness and ruin.
Christ, the great high priest who never failed the standard of faithful integrity Solomon was called to but did not keep.

study.oneStory: At the summit of Solomon's building achievements, God graciously confirms His presence in the temple while attaching the same covenant condition that has run through Israel's whole story since Sinai, blessing follows faithfulness. The chapter's closing survey of towns traded, labor conscripted, and gold imported already hints, in the narrator's understated way, that Solomon's own heart will not remain fully tamim, setting up the account of decline still to come. The whole passage points forward to the one King whose obedience never wavers and whose throne, unlike Solomon's, is established forever without condition, Jesus Christ, great David's greater Son.

quiz.q: What did the LORD confirm to Solomon in His second appearance, verse 3?

options.text: That He had consecrated the temple by putting His Name there forever, with His eyes and heart there for all time.
options.note: Verse 3 states this directly as God's answer to Solomon's dedication prayer.

options.text: That the temple would only remain for one generation regardless of Solomon's conduct.
options.note: The temple's permanence is affirmed in verse 3, though later verses attach a condition regarding faithfulness, not a fixed one generation limit.

options.text: That Solomon should build a second temple in Gibeon to replace the one in Jerusalem.
options.note: Gibeon was the site of the LORD's first appearance, chapter 3, not a call to build a second temple there.

options.text: The LORD gave no response at all to Solomon's dedication prayer.
options.note: This entire chapter is the LORD's direct response, beginning in verse 3.

quiz.q: What condition does the LORD attach to establishing Solomon's throne forever, verses 4-5?

options.text: That Solomon walk before God with a heart of integrity and uprightness, as David did, keeping His statutes and ordinances.
options.note: Verses 4-5 state this condition explicitly, tying the throne's permanence to covenant faithfulness.

options.text: That Solomon build an even larger temple than the one already completed.
options.note: The temple is already complete and accepted, verse 3; the stated condition is faithfulness of heart, not further building.

options.text: That Solomon defeat all of Israel's remaining enemies in battle.
options.note: Military conquest is not the condition given; the LORD's condition concerns covenant obedience and integrity of heart.

options.text: No condition is attached; the throne is established unconditionally regardless of Solomon's conduct.
options.note: Verses 6-9 explicitly describe consequences for unfaithfulness, showing the promise is conditional.

quiz.q: What warning does the LORD give if Solomon or his sons turn to worship other gods, verses 6-9?

options.text: God will cut off Israel from the land, banish the temple from His presence, and make Israel an object of scorn among the nations.
options.note: Verses 6-9 spell out this exact warning, later fulfilled at the exile.

options.text: God promises to overlook idolatry so long as the temple sacrifices continue as scheduled.
options.note: The warning is the opposite; ritual continuity does not exempt Israel from the consequences of idolatry.

options.text: God warns that only Solomon personally, not his descendants, would face any consequence.
options.note: Verse 6 explicitly includes 'you or your sons,' extending the warning beyond Solomon alone.

options.text: The LORD gives no warning about the consequences of unfaithfulness in this chapter.
options.note: Verses 6-9 contain an extensive, explicit warning.

quiz.q: Why was Hiram displeased with the towns Solomon gave him, verses 12-13?

options.text: He inspected the twenty towns in Galilee and was not pleased with them, calling the region the Land of Cabul.
options.note: Verses 12-13 state this directly, including the naming of the towns Cabul.

options.text: Solomon refused to give Hiram any towns at all despite Hiram's service.
options.note: Solomon did give Hiram twenty towns, verse 11; the issue was Hiram's dissatisfaction with their quality, not a refusal to give anything.

options.text: Hiram was displeased because the towns were located outside Solomon's kingdom entirely.
options.note: The towns were in the land of Galilee, within Solomon's territory, not outside it.

options.text: The text gives no reason for Hiram's reaction.
options.note: Verses 12-13 give a clear account of his inspection and dissatisfaction.

quiz.q: What does Solomon's fleet accomplish at the end of the chapter, verses 26-28?

options.text: It sails from Ezion-geber to Ophir, with Hiram's experienced sailors aboard, and imports 420 talents of gold for Solomon.
options.note: Verses 26-28 describe this exact trading voyage and its substantial gold import.

options.text: It is lost at sea and never returns with any goods.
options.note: The text records a successful voyage that delivers gold to Solomon, not a loss.

options.text: It is used exclusively for military conquest rather than trade.
options.note: The fleet's purpose here is trade, importing gold from Ophir, not military conquest.

options.text: Solomon builds no fleet of ships in this chapter.
options.note: Verse 26 explicitly describes Solomon assembling a fleet at Ezion-geber.

questions: The LORD confirms His presence in the temple but ties Solomon's future to a wholehearted walk of integrity, not to the building itself. Where might you be trusting in a structure, routine, or achievement rather than in ongoing faithfulness to God?
God calls Solomon to walk with a heart of tamim, integrity, as David did. What would it look like for your private life to match your public devotion this week?
The warning of exile and ruin came long before it happened, giving Israel time to turn back. Is there a warning in your own life right now that you have been slow to heed?
Hiram was quietly dissatisfied with what Solomon gave him, a small crack in an otherwise glowing account. Where in your own life might small compromises be building toward larger consequences?

Solomon's achievements, towns, labor, ships, and gold, are impressive yet framed by God's one essential question, faithfulness of heart. What single achievement are you tempted to let stand in for genuine devotion to God?

translationNotes.note: The BSB footnote notes that some Septuagint manuscripts, Syriac, and Arabic read this temple has become a heap of rubble, while the Hebrew reads though this temple is now exalted; the BSB and 2 Chronicles 7:21 support the rendering of ruin, fitting the warning's context of judgment.
The BSB footnote explains Cabul sounds like the Hebrew for good-for-nothing, preserving Hiram's blunt dissatisfaction with the towns as a permanent place name.
The BSB footnote notes the Hebrew term rendered supporting terraces is literally the Millo, a term referring to a filled terrace structure supporting Jerusalem's defenses, also mentioned in verse 24.

The BSB footnote explains that forms of the Hebrew cherem, rendered devote to destruction, refer to giving over persons or things to the LORD, whether by destruction or as an offering, the background for why some Canaanite peoples remained to be conscripted as laborers.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Kings 9:1-28. All em dashes and en dashes present in the source (for example in verses 9, 21, and 28) were converted to commas; none remain anywhere in this file.
